items.hook: Happy to help you skip the menu spiral.
items.bridge: With fast food, the quickest shortcut is usually whether this is lunch, dinner, a snack, or a group order.
items.next_step: Ask whether this is for one person or a group and whether they want pickup, delivery, or dine-in.
items.handoff_suggestion: Menu-guidance opportunity if the user answers with meal context.

items.hook: Makes sense to optimize for speed if your break is tight.
items.bridge: For a quick-service meal, the store, pickup versus delivery, and item count matter more than a blanket ready-time promise.
items.next_step: Ask for store, time, order mode, and roughly how many items.
items.handoff_suggestion: High-intent order if timing and store are confirmed.

items.hook: That sounds like a do-not-make-me-think dinner moment.
items.bridge: For an after-work order, the most useful split is usually solo meal versus two-person share and pickup versus delivery.
items.next_step: Ask how many people are eating and whether they want something quick to carry home or delivered.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good conversion signal if the user confirms group size and channel.

items.hook: That already sounds like a real coordination order, not casual browsing.
items.bridge: For a fast-food bulk order, timing, meal count, packaging, and invoice needs usually matter as much as the menu itself.
items.next_step: Ask for store, delivery window, meal count, and invoice requirement.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route as a high-intent operational lead.

items.hook: Good call to check breakfast timing before you leave home.
items.bridge: Breakfast availability can shift by store and pickup time, especially on commute-heavy locations.
items.next_step: Ask which store and what time they want to pick up or arrive.
items.handoff_suggestion: Breakfast-order opportunity if store and timing are confirmed.

items.hook: Ordering for adults and kids together is easier if we keep it simple.
items.bridge: For a mixed group, spice level, shareable sides, and whether you want separate meals matter more than chasing every popular item.
items.next_step: Ask how many adults and kids are eating and whether they want separate meals or shared sides.
items.handoff_suggestion: Menu-guidance opportunity for a family order.

items.hook: Makes sense to check the offer before you place the order.
items.bridge: Fast-food deals often depend on store, time slot, and whether you order in app, in person, or by delivery.
items.next_step: Ask for store and order channel.
items.handoff_suggestion: Useful bridge into ordering if the user confirms details.

items.hook: Happy to help narrow that down before you make the trip.
items.bridge: Limited items can change by store and by time of day, so the fastest useful answer starts there.
items.next_step: Ask for the store and whether they want it now or later today.

items.hook: That is worth checking on the exact item instead of guessing broadly.
items.bridge: With quick-service orders, sauce swaps, removed ingredients, and side changes can vary by product and store flow.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact item and the exact change they want.

items.bridge: Ingredient and build details should be treated carefully item by item, especially when the restriction matters more than taste.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact restriction, the item they are considering, and the store if known.
items.handoff_suggestion: Flag for careful confirmation if the restriction affects the purchase decision.

items.hook: I am glad you asked before placing the order.
items.bridge: Allergy questions should be handled as safety checks, not casual menu advice.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact allergen and the item, then escalate for confirmation.

items.hook: I get why you do not want to wait around without a real signal.
items.bridge: Delivery timing depends on store load, rider flow, distance, and how many items are in the order.
items.next_step: Ask for store, timing target, delivery or pickup mode, and order size.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route quickly if the user is close to ordering.

items.hook: I can understand why that would feel frustrating.
items.bridge: The fastest way to check a missing or damaged order is usually the store, order time, and a photo if there is one.
items.next_step: Move the user to DM for order details and photos.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate to after-sales or store operations.

items.bridge: With fast food, people often care about different proof points such as timing, texture, made-to-order feel, or whether pickup is the better route.
items.next_step: Invite the user to name the exact freshness or quality point they doubt.
items.handoff_suggestion: Trust-repair opportunity if the user gets more specific.

items.hook: That sounds like a keep-it-simple order.
items.bridge: For a late-night quick meal, the useful split is usually one-person versus shareable order and pickup versus delivery.
items.next_step: Ask how many people are eating and whether they want something quick to grab or delivered.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good conversion signal if the user confirms timing and order route.

items.hook: Happy to move faster on this.
items.bridge: The safest first step is still platform DM so order details, screenshots, or store questions do not get scattered in public.
items.next_step: Ask for the store plus order details in DM.
